reply.proposal: Introduce Express or similar framework abstraction layer with a dedicated middleware stack organizer. Migrate hand-rolled auth, rate limiting, and logging into composable, testable middleware units with a clear precedence system (logging → auth → rate limiting → business logic). Use industry-standard packages (e.g., passport for auth, express-rate-limit, morgan for logging) to eliminate duplication and reduce maintenance burden. Establish a middleware registry pattern so new engineers understand the execution order at a glance and can add middleware without breaking existing chains.

Implement Redux Toolkit as the centralized state management solution for the dashboard, organizing slices by feature domain (auth, analytics, ui, etc.) to maintain separation of concerns. Configure the store with Redux DevTools for debugging and use Redux Thunk for async operations. Create a migration plan to gradually move existing local state and prop-drilling patterns into Redux slices, starting with the most problematic areas like authentication and sidebar state. Set up middleware for logging and error handling, and establish clear conventions for action naming and selector organization to keep the codebase maintainable as the dashboard grows.

We'll install Prisma in packages/api with a schema.prisma file that defines our data models, then generate the Prisma Client for type-safe queries. This replaces raw SQL strings with a fluent, strongly-typed API. We'll set up a database connection via .env.local, run migrations with `prisma migrate dev` to keep schema and database in sync, and commit the schema file so the team shares the same model definitions. The generated client will be imported throughout our API routes, giving us IDE autocomplete and catching query errors at build time.

We should use axios as our HTTP client with a custom wrapper that implements exponential backoff retries, configurable timeouts, and structured error logging that captures response status, headers, and error context. This wrapper will be a shared utility in packages/api that all billing provider calls route through, ensuring consistent behavior and making it easy to add observability hooks or circuit breaker patterns later. We'll configure sensible defaults (3 retries, 5s timeout) but allow per-request overrides for specific billing endpoints that may need different behavior.
key_technologies: typescript (for strong typing of retry config and error responses)

reply.proposal: Store the session token in memory (JavaScript variable) as the primary approach, with sessionStorage as a fallback for page refreshes, but never localStorage to avoid XSS persistence. Configure the authentication endpoint to set an HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie as well, allowing the browser to automatically attach it to same-origin requests. For fetch calls that need explicit token attachment, read from the in-memory store and add to the Authorization header; the dual approach gives us both automatic cookie handling for navigation and explicit control for API calls. Implement a token refresh mechanism that updates the in-memory store before expiration, triggered by a background timer or interceptor on 401 responses.
acknowledged_rejections: localStorage for tokens (XSS attack surface - persists stolen tokens across sessions)
Storing tokens solely in HttpOnly cookies without JavaScript access (conflicts with stated requirement that SPA attach tokens itself)

reply.proposal: I recommend installing Jest as a dev dependency in packages/api and configuring it with a jest.config.js that's tailored for our Node.js/Express environment, including proper module resolution and test file patterns. We should set up a basic test structure under a __tests__ directory mirroring our src layout, and add test scripts to package.json for running tests, coverage reports, and watch mode. This gives us immediate capability to write unit tests for request handlers using Jest's built-in mocking and assertion library without additional dependencies.

Implement CSS Modules as the styling approach for the new marketing pages, allowing each component to have a co-located .module.css file that scopes styles automatically and prevents naming conflicts. This aligns with the designer's preference for component-scoped styles while maintaining consistency with modern React patterns. We'll establish a clear file structure convention (e.g., Button.tsx paired with Button.module.css) and document class naming guidelines to ensure maintainability. CSS Modules provide zero-runtime overhead, work seamlessly with our existing build setup, and make it trivial to track which styles are used by which components.
key_technologies: CSS custom properties (for shared design tokens)

reply.proposal: Implement a Redis-backed distributed cache for session data with a write-through strategy to ensure consistency across API instances. Sessions will be cached with TTL matching the session expiration time, with cache invalidation on logout. We'll use connection pooling to minimize overhead and add cache-aside logic in our session middleware to check Redis before hitting the primary session store. This approach provides sub-millisecond lookups while maintaining data consistency without requiring application-level coordination.
key_technologies: Connection pooling library native to driver
Session middleware layer (express-session, Flask-Session, or custom)
Consistent hashing if multi-node Redis setup needed

reply.proposal: Implement schema-driven migrations using Prisma ORM as our schema source-of-truth, which provides automatic migration generation via `prisma migrate dev` and tracks migration history in a dedicated table. This eliminates hand-written SQL for most schema changes while maintaining database-agnostic definitions. We'll establish a workflow where developers modify the Prisma schema, Prisma generates the migration file, and we review the generated SQL before deploying to avoid unexpected changes. For complex migrations that Prisma cannot auto-generate, we'll use its manual migration feature to write SQL only when necessary.

Implement a GraphQL layer as a public API facade that wraps existing REST endpoints, allowing mobile clients to query specific fields with fine-grained control. GraphQL's strongly-typed schema will be auto-generated from our REST service contracts, and we'll use Apollo Server to handle the GraphQL gateway. This eliminates overfetching without requiring REST endpoint proliferation, gives clients declarative control over response shape, and provides excellent mobile developer experience. We'll phase this in with a `/graphql` endpoint running alongside REST, allowing gradual migration of mobile clients.
key_technologies: node-fetch or axios (for wrapping REST calls)

cells.context:
Project conventions and prior decisions:
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
- Structured logging with pino, one JSON line per request — Fast, structured, and the ops dashboards parse JSON lines natively
- CI runs on GitHub Actions with a single reusable workflow — One workflow file, matrix per package; no CI vendor lock beyond what the repo host already implies
- pnpm workspace for the monorepo — Strict node_modules layout catches phantom dependencies; workspace protocol keeps internal packages linked
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- All API errors use the problem+json envelope — RFC 7807 gives clients one error shape; internal codes map to documented types
- Local development runs through docker compose — Postgres and the API start with one command; parity with the deploy image
- Prettier with the shared config; ESLint flat config for lint rules — Formatting is not reviewed; lint rules are; keeping the tools separate keeps both fast
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- User file uploads go to S3-compatible object storage, never the database — Keeps the database small and backups fast; presigned URLs offload transfer
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Each package keeps a README with run and test instructions — Onboarding doc lives next to the code it describes
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now

reply.proposal: Refactor the middleware stack into a composable set of Hono middlewares that live in a dedicated packages/api/src/middleware directory, each handling one concern (auth, rate limiting, logging, error handling) with explicit ordering documented at the router level. Build a thin middleware factory that accepts config (rate limit plan, log level, auth strategy) so each route can declare its requirements declaratively rather than wrapping handlers. Use Hono's built-in middleware composition and c.get() context passing to share parsed tokens and rate-limit state across handlers without ceremony, and add integration tests that verify middleware order and that concerns don't leak (e.g., rate limit headers present even on auth failures).
key_technologies: pino for structured logging (already in use)
TypeScript function overloads for type-safe middleware factories
acknowledged_rejections: Express or other framework migration — Hono's edge-runtime compatibility and minimal overhead are core to the stack
External middleware packages like helmet or morgan — Hand-written middleware keeps the auth and rate-limit logic visible and co-located with business rules
Middleware as decorators or annotations — Declarative routing via factory functions is more testable and visible in the call site than decorator syntax

reply.proposal: Redux Toolkit would add ceremony and bundle weight that contradicts our stated constraints. Instead, keep Zustand and refactor the store into separate domain-focused modules (session.ts, filters.ts, toasts.ts, etc.) with explicit action creators and selectors colocated with each slice. Add a simple debug middleware to log state transitions in development and keep the entire global state tree under 200 lines. If the state surface grows beyond what Zustand handles cleanly, we'll revisit; right now the constraint is cognitive load during changes, not the library.
key_technologies: TypeScript type guards for state mutations
Immer plugin (Zustand built-in) for immutable update patterns
acknowledged_rejections: Redux Toolkit — adds boilerplate (actions, reducers, selectors as separate files) and bundle size; our global state is small and Zustand's functional model fits the current scale
MobX — implicit reactivity makes mutations harder to trace in our small state; explicit Zustand subscriptions align better with the team's preference for visible data flow

reply.proposal: We should not adopt Prisma. Our team has explicitly chosen postgres.js with hand-written SQL because query plans remain visible and the abstraction layer is removed—both critical for the performance-sensitive endpoints we're building. Prisma's query builder would reintroduce the runtime abstraction we rejected, and its migrations would conflict with our forward-only numbered SQL migrations that are reviewed as literal schema changes. The type safety Prisma offers is already covered by our typed row mappers colocated with each query, which give us the same compile-time guarantees without hiding the SQL execution model.
acknowledged_rejections: Prisma ORM

reply.proposal: Use the existing native fetch with the small retry wrapper we already have—it handles retries, timeouts, and JSON parsing in ~40 lines. Wrap the billing provider's API responses in a Zod schema at the call site to validate shape and map their error format into our problem+json envelope; this keeps error reporting consistent and surfaces schema mismatches early. Add OpenTelemetry span tracking around the fetch call so billing requests appear in traces and we can debug provider latency without adding logging noise. Store the provider's base URL and API key in environment variables, validated at startup so we fail fast if config is missing.
acknowledged_rejections: axios or other HTTP client library—we standardized on native fetch to avoid a dependency and keep the stack minimal
dedicated billing SDK—if the provider publishes one, we can evaluate it later; for now, REST + Zod gives us control and visibility
retry logic in a separate package—the existing wrapper is proven; extracting it would fragment ownership

reply.proposal: Keep tokens in httpOnly cookies for the initial session and let the browser send them automatically on same-origin requests; if the mobile web team needs to read tokens in JavaScript for cross-origin calls or custom headers, issue a separate non-httpOnly cookie alongside the httpOnly one that mirrors the token value, marked SameSite=Lax and Secure. The SPA reads the mirror cookie only when needed (e.g., for Authorization headers on explicit outbound requests) and never stores it in localStorage. This preserves the httpOnly cookie's XSS protection for most traffic while giving the team the token reference they need without compromising the existing architecture.
key_technologies: httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies (existing session token storage)
Non-httpOnly SameSite=Lax Secure cookie (mirror token for JavaScript read)
native fetch with existing retry wrapper
acknowledged_rejections: Storing tokens in localStorage or sessionStorage — violates the XSS-hardening decision to keep tokens out of JavaScript scope
Removing httpOnly and making all cookies readable — loses the XSS protection that motivated the original decision
Adding a flag service or token-exchange endpoint — adds complexity beyond mirroring a second cookie
Using Authorization header injection middleware in the SPA — couples token handling to every fetch call instead of relying on browser defaults

We've already committed to zero test-framework dependencies across the monorepo, and node:test runs TypeScript output directly without transform config—Jest would introduce duplicate test infrastructure, a second test runner, and maintenance burden for no functional gain on handler tests. acknowledged_rejections: Jest as a test framework—conflicts with the established node:test convention and adds unnecessary complexity to CI and local workflows

reply.proposal: Implement an in-process LRU cache in each API instance using a simple Map-based implementation with a TTL of 5 minutes and a 1000-entry limit, keyed by session token hash. On cache miss, query the UNLOGGED session table and populate the cache; invalidate on logout by deleting the Postgres row (which cascades cache coherence across instances on next lookup). This keeps the single-datastore constraint, adds zero external dependencies, and trades bounded memory per instance for eliminating redundant Postgres round-trips during a typical user's request burst. Instrument cache hits/misses in structured logs and OpenTelemetry traces so ops can tune the TTL and size.
key_technologies: node:zlib (optional, for token hash compression in logs only)
OpenTelemetry API (already in use) for cache hit/miss spans
acknowledged_rejections: Redis or Memcached — adds a third datastore and operational burden; the spike showed Postgres UNLOGGED tables already meet latency SLA
Distributed cache invalidation protocol — overkill for session lookups; eventual consistency within 5 minutes is acceptable given logout hard-deletes the row
External flag service for cache TTL tuning — feature flags already deploy with code; embed TTL as an env var like CACHE_SESSION_TTL_MS

reply.proposal: This conflicts with our deliberate choice to hand-write, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations so every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL and prod history stays linear. Auto-generated migrations hide the actual SQL from review, risk generating inefficient plans, and make it harder to reason about what deployed. Instead, lean into the current flow: keep a schema.sql file as the source of truth (reviewed once), and continue writing numbered migrations for diffs. If the pain is volume, invest in a migration generator that outputs SQL for review before commit—not one that bypasses the review gate.

Liquibase or Flyway schema diffing (adds a second schema definition source and vendor lock)
Atlas or similar schema-as-code tools with auto-generation (same review bypass and adds migration platform dependency)

reply.proposal: Implement sparse fieldsets as a standard query parameter (`?fields=id,name,email`) across all REST endpoints, validated by Zod schemas that define which fields are selectable per resource type. Each endpoint's response schema already lives in Zod and doubles as the OpenAPI source; we extend it with a `fields` param that filters the serialized JSON before the response leaves the handler. This keeps the approach REST-native (HTTP caching respects the query string), requires no new infrastructure, and integrates seamlessly with our existing Zod-to-OpenAPI pipeline. Document the feature once in the API README and update the OpenAPI spec generator to show available fields per resource.
key_technologies: postgres.js (no query changes; filtering happens in the response layer)
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL — adds a second query language and introspection surface; REST with sparse fieldsets covers the use case within our existing OpenAPI contract
Redis caching layer for field-specific payloads — we benchmarked the session cache pattern; row-level caching complicates invalidation without reducing database load on the mobile teams' behalf

Columnar projection at the SQL layer — postgres.js queries are hand-written and visible; pushing field selection into SQL would scatter the filtering logic and make the OpenAPI contract harder to derive from code

reply.proposal: Refactor the hand-rolled middleware into a composable Hono middleware stack using Hono's built-in helpers (auth, logger, cors) and our existing pino for structured logging, keeping the same Hono foundation and edge-runtime compatibility. Extract middleware concerns into named, ordered functions in a single middleware.ts file with explicit composition — auth first, then rate limiting, then logging — so the order is visible and testable. Integrate our token-bucket rate limiter and session lookup (already in Postgres) as dedicated middleware, and use Hono's problem+json error handler to ensure all errors conform to RFC 7807. This keeps the stack at zero new dependencies, maintains our audit trail of generated SQL, and makes middleware order and behavior reviewable in a single file.
key_technologies: Hono built-in middleware (logger, cors, bearer auth helpers)
pino (already in use for structured logging)
postgres.js driver (already in use for session lookups)
acknowledged_rejections: Express — rejected 2026-03; larger dependency surface and cold-start overhead
Fastify — no measured gain over Hono for our workload
Middleware orchestration frameworks (e.g., tinyhttp, polka) — unnecessary; Hono's middleware model already covers what we need
Separate rate-limiting service (e.g., Redis) — one datastore policy active until 10x traffic

reply.proposal: We are not adopting Prisma. Our 2026-02 spike measured Prisma's query engine adding 200–400ms to cold starts on serverless deploys, which directly conflicts with our edge-runtime requirement for Hono. The postgres.js driver with hand-written SQL and colocated TypeScript row mappers gives us type safety (via mapped types) without the abstraction tax. If type safety becomes a pain point, we can introduce a lightweight query builder (e.g. Kysely) that keeps SQL visible and compiles to strings at build time, but that remains a future decision.

reply.proposal: Reject the request and keep tokens in httpOnly cookies. The SPA does not need to read the token—the browser automatically attaches httpOnly cookies to fetch requests via the Credentials API (fetch(..., { credentials: 'include' })). Exposing tokens to JavaScript contradicts the 2026-04 pentest findings that demonstrated XSS exfiltration through localStorage. If the mobile web team needs to inspect tokens for debugging, provide a separate /debug/session endpoint (auth-walled, dev-only) that returns non-sensitive session metadata without exposing the raw token. Document the Credentials: 'include' requirement in the API README and the dashboard's fetch wrapper so every team member understands why tokens stay in cookies.
acknowledged_rejections: localStorage — XSS exfiltration vulnerability proven in 2026-04 pentest; analytics snippet theft scenario is live risk
sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage
Reading httpOnly cookies from JavaScript — By design, httpOnly prevents script access; this is the security property we chose

This keeps the stack at one datastore, avoids operational overhead, and aligns with our prior rejection of Redis. Add instrumentation to trace cache hit rate so we can revisit if a second datastore becomes justified at scale.

in-process memory cache without TTL — Our prior rejection noted it breaks under multiple instances; this proposal adds TTL-based invalidation and metrics to keep instances loosely synchronized and observable

reply.proposal: This conflicts with a hard-won decision from the 2026-03 incident where an auto-generated migration silently dropped a column and reached staging. We explicitly rejected auto-generated migrations after that rollback because generated DDL cannot be safely reviewed—the literal SQL is the contract. Instead, propose investing in migration helper tooling: a script that diffs your current schema against postgres.js type definitions and generates a skeleton migration file with TODOs for manual review. This keeps the safety property (every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL) while reducing the tedious boilerplate of writing CREATE TABLE statements from scratch.

reply.proposal: Implement sparse fieldsets via a query parameter (e.g., `?fields=id,name,email`) that clients can use to request only the columns they need, with validation and documentation baked into the existing Zod + OpenAPI setup. Each endpoint's Zod schema gains an optional `fields` input that whitelists requestable columns, the resolver projects the query SELECT list accordingly, and OpenAPI documents the allowed fields per endpoint. This stays within REST semantics, works with HTTP caching (vary on the fields param), and requires no new infrastructure—just a small utility function to transform Zod schemas into field validators and a query builder helper to apply projections to postgres.js queries. Roll this out endpoint-by-endpoint, starting with the mobile team's highest-traffic screens.
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem

reply.proposal: We should not adopt Prisma for this project. Our team made a deliberate decision in 2026-02 to use postgres.js with hand-written SQL specifically because query engine cold-start latency was a measured problem on serverless deploys, and we value visibility into generated SQL for performance tuning. Prisma's abstraction layer directly conflicts with both constraints. Instead, we can improve type safety within our current postgres.js + SQL approach by expanding our existing typed row mappers, adding a code-generation step from our OpenAPI schema to derive database types, or introducing a lightweight query builder like Kysely that preserves SQL visibility while adding type safety.
acknowledged_rejections: Prisma

reply.proposal: We cannot safely adopt auto-generated migrations given the 2026-03 incident where a generated diff silently dropped a column that reached staging before discovery. The team's prior decision to require hand-written, reviewed SQL migrations reflects hard-won operational safety—generated DDL cannot be trusted for schema changes on this project. Instead, we should invest in tooling that *assists* hand-written migrations: a schema linter that catches common mistakes before review, a migration validator that dry-runs against a shadow production schema, and clearer migration templates to reduce manual boilerplate. This preserves the reviewed SQL requirement while cutting the friction of authoring them.
key_technologies: Custom migration linter (in-house or lightweight open-source)
Shadow database for migration validation (Postgres standby or point-in-time clone)

down migrations — destructive changes cannot be reversed; restore-from-backup is the real rollback strategy

reply.proposal: Implement sparse fieldsets using standard query parameters (`?fields=id,name,email`) on all endpoints, allowing clients to specify exactly which fields to include in responses. This leverages HTTP semantics and OpenAPI can document the parameter clearly, making it discoverable for all client languages without adding resolver complexity or multiple datastore concerns. The API continues REST versioning and caching as-is; sparse fieldsets are trivially composable with existing httpOnly cookies and session caching. We can roll this out incrementally by endpoint and document it in the OpenAPI spec so mobile (and other clients) can adopt at their own pace.
key_technologies: OpenAPI 3.1 schema extensions to document sparse fieldset parameters
Query parameter parsing and field projection middleware (library-agnostic; implement in our API framework)
acknowledged_rejections: GraphQL — Already rejected 2026-05; resolver N+1 and cache invalidation overhead outweigh flexibility for ~30 endpoints
tRPC — Already rejected; locks public API contract to TypeScript ecosystem, breaks non-JS consumers
Dedicated field-selection query language or schema layer — Adds operational complexity and learning curve; sparse fieldsets solve the stated problem within REST
